elements.2412-15272_sent_76300cad: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive versatility across various tasks.
elements.2412-15272_sent_d7e13376: To eliminate its hallucinations, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has emerged as a powerful approach, leveraging external knowledge sources like knowledge graphs (KGs).
elements.2412-15272_sent_848495a5: In this paper, we study the task of KG-driven RAG and propose a novel Similar Graph Enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation (SimGRAG) method.
elements.2412-15272_sent_1777aa5d: It effectively addresses the challenge of aligning query texts and KG structures through a two-stage process: (1) query-to-pattern, which uses an LLM to transform queries into a desired graph pattern, and (2) pattern-to-subgraph, which quantifies the alignment between the pattern and candidate subgraphs using a graph semantic distance (GSD) metric.
elements.2412-15272_sent_a649055d: We also develop an optimized retrieval algorithm that efficiently identifies the top-$k$ subgraphs within 1-second latency on a 10-million-scale KG.

elements.2412-15272_sent_a5b6c8ea: As summarized in Table~\ref{tab: overall result}, supervised task-specific methods outperform KG-driven RAG approaches that require additional training, except on the MetaQA 1-hop dataset, where G-retriever achieves a 1\% performance gain.
elements.2412-15272_sent_ebd46870: Notably, supervised task-specific methods generally require smaller model sizes and lower training costs, making them a more cost-effective option in practice.
elements.2412-15272_sent_accf7b00: To avoid the costs of training, preparing samples, or serving a trained model, the inherent capabilities of pre-trained LLMs simplify the process, especially with the widely available APIs.
elements.2412-15272_sent_95c9e1f1: However, directly using LLMs leads to the poorest performance.
elements.2412-15272_sent_f4dcdd9b: Thus, it is necessary to develop KG-driven RAG methods that do not require additional training.
elements.2412-15272_sent_a0a27c50: Among these methods, the proposed SimGRAG approach demonstrates substantially higher Hits@1 and accuracy in most cases.
elements.2412-15272_sent_d09741bb: In fact, SimGRAG performs comparably to supervised task-specific models and even outperforms the supervised GEAR method on the FactKG dataset, while maintaining the plug-and-play usability.
elements.2412-15272_sent_c4185dda: Moreover, the performance gap between SimGRAG and other RAG approaches becomes larger as the complexity of the questions increases on the MetaQA dataset.
elements.2412-15272_sent_660242c2: As discussed in Section~\ref{sec: pattern-to-subgraph}, the combined use of graph isomorphism and semantic similarity effectively reduces noise and ensures conciseness, thus benefiting the performance of SimGRAG for 2-hop and 3-hop questions.
elements.2412-15272_sent_68212485: In contrast, KELP fails to support 3-hop paths due to the challenges of handling the vast number of potential paths with its trained path selection model.

elements.2412-15272_sent_f8b0847b: As the complexity of the queries increases in the MetaQA dataset, we also observe a higher incidence of errors in the subgraph-augmented generation step, since it is more difficult for the LLM to accurately extract relevant information for a complex question from the retrieved subgraphs.

elements.2412-15272_sent_b638eaf2: In this paper, we investigate the problem of KG-driven RAG and introduce a novel SimGRAG approach that effectively aligns query texts with KG structures.
elements.2412-15272_sent_5dfb3774: For query-to-pattern alignment, we employ an LLM to generate a pattern graph that aligns with the query text, ensuring plug-and-play usability without any training.
elements.2412-15272_sent_fba38063: For pattern-to-subgraph alignment, we introduce the Graph Semantic Distance (GSD) metric to quantify the alignment between the desired pattern and the underlying subgraphs in the KG, ensuring context conciseness and preventing entity leaks.
elements.2412-15272_sent_80ddb463: Additionally, we propose an optimized algorithm to retrieve the top-$k$ similar subgraphs with the smallest GSD, improving retrieval efficiency and scalability.
elements.2412-15272_sent_8b3a50aa: Extensive experiments demonstrate that SimGRAG consistently outperforms existing KG-driven RAG approaches.
elements.2412-15272_sent_80e4f635: Since the proposed SimGRAG method is designed to offer plug-and-play usability, its performance is closely tied to the underlying capabilities of the large language model (LLM).
elements.2412-15272_sent_890ebca3: Specifically, the method relies heavily on the ability of LLMs to understand and follow instructions effectively in both steps of the query-to-pattern alignment and verbalized subgraph-augmented generation.
elements.2412-15272_sent_dac7f8d9: Thus, the performance of SimGRAG can be substantially degraded when utilizing lower-quality or less capable LLMs, especially in scenarios involving more complex queries that demand advanced reasoning skills.

elements.2412-15272_sent_6390b5b5: We manually categorize all the encountered errors of the SimGRAG method in our experiments.
elements.2412-15272_sent_313ff5d2: The errors occurring during the query-to-pattern alignment step are defined as: LLM fails to follow the given instructions and examples.
elements.2412-15272_sent_be24df88: For example, for the query ``The lady Anne Monson was born in the Darlington location of the ITL?'' from FactKG dataset, the LLM gives the pattern graph with only one triple ``('Anne Monson', 'born in', 'Darlington')'', which is not aligned with the query text.
elements.2412-15272_sent_d5870a17: The error occurred during the subgraph-augmented generation step is defined as that given the correct retrieved subgraph, the LLM fails to provide the final correct response.
elements.2412-15272_sent_6822cfec: For example, for the question ``what films did Lucky McKee star in'' from the MetaQA dataset, correct subgraphs of ``[('Lucky McKee', 'starred\_actors', 'Roman')]'' is successfully retrieved, along with the two subgraphs with lower GSD (``[('Lucky McKee', 'directed\_by', 'All Cheerleaders Die')]'' and ``[('Lucky McKee', 'directed\_by', 'May')]'').
elements.2412-15272_sent_66902fb0: However, the LLM gives the final response of ``According to the evidences, there is no direct connection between Lucky McKee and a film they starred in.
elements.2412-15272_sent_c03732a4: The graphs only mention that Lucky McKee directed films ('All Cheerleaders Die' and 'May'), but do not provide information about the films they acted in.'' Errors occurring during the pattern-to-subgraph alignment phase are defined as: LLM follows the given instructions and examples to generate a satisfactory pattern graph, but the retrieval algorithm fails to retrieve the ground-truth subgraph for the query.
elements.2412-15272_sent_f2ac3402: It is because the ground-truth subgraphs have different structures and thus cannot be successfully aligned with the ground-truth subgraphs.
elements.2412-15272_sent_49a7c28c: For example, for the query ``A food is classed as a Dessert and can be served warm (freshly baked) or cold.'', the LLM-generated pattern graph is ``[('UNKNOWN food 1', 'classed as', 'Dessert'), ('UNKNOWN food 1', 'served', '"warm"'), ('UNKNOWN food 1', 'served', '"cold"')]''.
elements.2412-15272_sent_f2c73e3e: However, the ground-truth subgraphs have the structure like ``[('The food name', 'classed as', 'Dessert'), ('The food name', 'served', '"warm (freshly baked) or cold"')]''.

elements.2412-15272_sent_625dfcd0: However, both KG-GPT \cite{kggpt} and KELP \cite{KELP} expand subgraphs or paths from the user-provided oracle entities, while G-Retriever \cite{G-retriever} restricts the KG to a 2-hop oracle entity neighborhood.
elements.2412-15272_sent_d0d6bd54: In other words, they need to know which entities are exactly correct before running, and the search space will be constrained in the ground truth area, thereby reducing the problem hardness.
elements.2412-15272_sent_0bb753b9: Though all methods will work better with the oracle entities, experimental evaluation in Table~\ref{tab: overall result} shows that even when we allow certain baselines to benefit from using the oracle entities, their performance still underperforms the SimGRAG method that does not require such entities.
elements.2412-15272_sent_1c264932: In other words, if we do not provide them for such baselines, their performance may degrade further.
elements.2412-15272_sent_75e0756c: Moreover, it is intuitive to use the results of a top-$k$ entity linker as a substitute for the oracle entity in certain baselines.
elements.2412-15272_sent_193057f3: However, it would significantly increase the computational complexity and latency, since these methods might need to run the entire pipeline independently for each candidate entity.
elements.2412-15272_sent_7df68641: In contrast, the SimGRAG method naturally avoids relying oracle entities without such independent redundant computations.
elements.2412-15272_sent_20408376: Furthermore, unlike existing approaches, the internal mechanism of SimGRAG method is designed to better handle and filter out those noisy entities in real-world KGs.
elements.2412-15272_sent_5893e67e: As discussed in Section~\ref{sec: pattern-to-subgraph}, we use the Graph Semantic Distance (GSD) metric, which can effectively incorporate somewhat distant entities or relations that still contribute valuable evidence to the overall subgraph.
elements.2412-15272_sent_220f856d: For example, Figure~\ref{fig: semantic similarity} shows that a candidate entity whose semantic distance is ranked $112$ can still be used in the retrieved subgraph.
elements.2412-15272_sent_5a3cf6ca: Generally, the proposed SimGRAG method is closer to the ideal feature through a carefully designed mechanism, while ensuring better performance than the baseline methods.
elements.2412-15272_sent_93d3218b: For example, for the query ``The lady Anne Monson was born in the Darlington location of the ITL?'' from FactKG dataset, the LLM gives the pattern graph with only one triple ``(`Anne Monson', `born in', `Darlington')'', which is not aligned with the query text.
elements.2412-15272_sent_979e2ebf: For example, for the question ``what films did Lucky McKee star in'' from the MetaQA dataset, correct subgraphs of ``[(`Lucky McKee', `starred\_actors', `Roman')]'' is successfully retrieved, along with the two subgraphs with lower GSD (``[(`Lucky McKee', `directed\_by', `All Cheerleaders Die')]'' and ``[(`Lucky McKee', `directed\_by', `May')]'').
elements.2412-15272_sent_e1fa37b9: The graphs only mention that Lucky McKee directed films (`All Cheerleaders Die' and `May'), but do not provide information about the films they acted in.'' Errors occurring during the pattern-to-subgraph alignment phase are defined as: LLM follows the given instructions and examples to generate a satisfactory pattern graph, but the retrieval algorithm fails to retrieve the ground-truth subgraph for the query.
elements.2412-15272_sent_e892e91c: For example, for the query ``A food is classed as a Dessert and can be served warm (freshly baked) or cold.'', the LLM-generated pattern graph is ``[(`UNKNOWN food 1', `classed as', `Dessert'), (`UNKNOWN food 1', `served', `"warm"'), (`UNKNOWN food 1', `served', `"cold"')]''.
elements.2412-15272_sent_762b5375: However, the ground-truth subgraphs have the structure like ``[(`The food name', `classed as', `Dessert'), (`The food name', `served', `"warm (freshly baked) or cold"')]''.
elements.2412-15272_sent_0b578e39: Following the previous study \cite{WC2014}, all queries (i.e., the pattern structure) in our experiments can be categorized into the following six types.
elements.2412-15272_sent_62a9dec3: For simplicity, we focus on topological structures and ignore the edge directions.
elements.2412-15272_sent_5894797c: 1-hop Path: Find an edge from a known subject $s$ to another entity $e$.
elements.2412-15272_sent_46c9be03: 2-hop Path: Find a 2-hop path from a known subject $s$ to another known or unknown entity $e$.
elements.2412-15272_sent_d6b0b54d: 3-hop Path: Find a 3-hop path from a known subject $s$ to another known or unknown entity $e$.
elements.2412-15272_sent_f0d29151: 2-hop Conjunction: Find two distinct edges that link two known subjects $s_1$ and $s_2$ with an unknown entity $e$.
elements.2412-15272_sent_eb436773: 3-hop Conjunction: Find an edge that links an known subject $s_1$ with an unknown entity $e$, as well as a 2-hop path that connects another known subjects $s_2$ with the same entity $e$.
elements.2412-15272_sent_8cdfd9fd: 3-hop Star: Find three distinct edges that links to the same known or unknown entities $e$.
elements.2412-15272_sent_91d81a10: We also test SimGRAG on the WebQuestionSP (WebQSP) dataset \cite{WebQSP} using WikiData \cite{wikidata}, which is the most popular and active KG.
elements.2412-15272_sent_527b7b54: We use the 2015 WikiData dump to align with WebQSP's creation time, which contains 26 million nodes and 57 million edges.
elements.2412-15272_sent_7b0960ff: We use the WebQSP-WD test set \cite{WebQSP-WD}, a corrected version of the original WebQSP dataset for WikiData compatibility.
elements.2412-15272_sent_dcb42350: Since \cite{WebQSP-WD} mentioned that not all queries are guaranteed answerable with WikiData, we manually verified each query in the test set, excluding those without any supporting evidence in WikiData.
elements.2412-15272_sent_e2a835e2: Specifically, each question in the WebQSP-WD test set is associated with a set of topic entities and answer entities, which were mapped from their original Freebase IDs to WikiData IDs.
elements.2412-15272_sent_bdcfb791: Since WebQSP dataset is all about questions within 2-hops \cite{G-retriever}, for each question, we extracted the 2-hop neighborhood of the topic entities, as well as the 2-hop neighborhood of the answer entities in WikiData KG, and union all these edges together to form a single subgraph.
elements.2412-15272_sent_80502504: To determine whether a question is supported by WikiData, we compared the question with its corresponding merged subgraph.
elements.2412-15272_sent_d98954a8: A question was considered unsupported if either of the following conditions held:
elements.2412-15272_sent_48b18599: None of the topic entities are connected to any answer entities within the subgraph.
elements.2412-15272_sent_05d6dce0: All connections between the topic entities and the answer entities are completely irrelevant to the intent of the question.
elements.2412-15272_sent_e1992216: Note that we never require the subgraph to contain a path that directly answers the question.
elements.2412-15272_sent_a51fef3a: As long as a human could infer the correct answer by reasoning over the whole connected subgraph, we considered the question to be supported.
elements.2412-15272_sent_b0e6cca1: After manually ensuring the quality and reliability of the test set, the proposed SimGRAG method achieves the Hits@1 of 87.7\%.
